aboutBlock.tagline: Middle+ Frontend Developer at Yandex · full-cycle product engineer (React · TypeScript · Node.js)
aboutBlock.desc:
Hi 👋 My name is Sergey, {{exp}} in frontend. Currently at Yandex, on the Yandex 360 for Business product (admin.yandex.ru).

~85% of my time goes to product features that grow functionality and bring the business money — and I own them end-to-end: from design and server-side SSR/BFF logic (Node.js) to production rollout. The rest is engineering initiatives on my own: CI speed-ups, performance, the design system, dev tooling.

This portfolio site is built on my work stack — a living example of what I do.

block1.desc: I own features end-to-end: from design to production. Not just the client — I write server-side SSR/BFF logic in Node.js and integrations with adjacent services.

block2.desc: Complex UIs in React + TypeScript, state management (Redux/Zustand/Effector). Contributions to design systems and UI kits, reusable components.

block3.desc: Figma prototyping, adaptive layouts, micro-interactions (Framer Motion), WCAG principles. Attentive to details and copy — 'pretty' ≠ 'usable'.

block4.desc: Git and code review, CI/CD pipelines, ESLint/Prettier, performance (APDEX, TTFB, Lighthouse). Testing: Playwright, Jest + RTL.

block1.name: Gleb Kuznetsov
block1.review: Sergey is incredibly easy to work with 🔥 He closes tasks fast and with quality, sometimes even ahead of QA — you can tell he thinks a task through instead of just writing code and tossing it over the fence. If bugs do show up, they don't survive half a day. He also writes great autotests, which is a rare talent for a frontender. I'd happily work with him more.

block2.name: Timur Safin
block2.review: Sergey was super responsive when I asked for help contributing to our service — with his help we closed the task noticeably faster. Thanks!

block3.name: Alisa Kovaleva
block3.review: A responsible and reliable developer who pays real attention to copy — that's genuinely great. Always reachable: happy to hop on a call, walk through a flow, discuss texts and design. Even on tight deadlines he makes edits carefully, never mixing anything up. Communication is comfortable and transparent — as a UX writer I'm very happy with the quality of his work 😃

block4.name: Anton Lebedev
block4.review: Working with you on projects is really great! Easy to communicate, you dive in well and are always ready to dig into a question instead of brushing it off. One wish — don't work on weekends 😄

block5.name: Pavel Morozov
block5.review: A real pleasure to deal with. Within a project, communication is easy: he explains complex things clearly and lays out risks and timelines. What to improve? Nothing really — all great.

block1.h: static_override: debug local frontend static on a real prestable pod, no rebuild

block3.h: Sped up team CI: −30% p90, ~2400 hrs/year saved

block4.h: Design system: components from scratch via Figma + UI migration

block1.desc:
Building the B2B admin panel of Yandex 360 for Business (admin.yandex.ru) in a product frontend team. I own product features end-to-end: design, alignment with adjacent teams, development and production rollout.

Product work (~85% of my time) — growing functionality and business money:
• Delivered large cross-service features turnkey: new registration flows, add-on activation, referral and promo mechanics — part already in production.
• I own features end-to-end: frontend + server-side SSR/BFF logic (Node.js) + integrations with adjacent services, not just the client.
• Cut TTM for new locales by 5–7% and Dev Time by 20–40% via modular refactoring.
• Contributed components from scratch to the corporate design system (via Figma) and migrated the UI to it at scale.

Engineering initiatives (beyond product tasks, on my own):
• static_override: enabled debugging local frontend static directly on a real prestable pod — a built pod serves static from my dev machine, no rebuild or redeploy; cut the debug loop dramatically.
• Moved frequently-changing data out of the release cycle — updated without a release; reused across 4+ features.
• Sped up the team's PR flow: test-stand provisioning −19% (p50) / −30% (p90), ~2400 hrs/year saved.
• Tuned a heavy SSR endpoint from ~2.9s to ~200ms (−90%), raised APDEX.
• Analytics dashboards (ClickHouse + DataLens), a talk at an internal frontend conference, team ESLint rules from an analysis of 2000+ review comments.

Stack: React, TypeScript, Node.js (SSR/BFF), Redux, design system, CI/CD, Playwright/Jest, ClickHouse/DataLens

block2.desc:
Developed a multi-page Next.js SPA: SSR/SSG, dynamic routes, multilingual support (i18n).

Implemented auth and a role-based access system (JWT), interactive dashboards with charts (Chart.js), custom UI components. Set up CI/CD on GitHub Actions.

Stack: Next.js, React, TypeScript, Redux Toolkit, Vite, MUI, CSS Modules

block3.desc:
Built an admin panel for a mobile app together with a Middle+ frontend developer.

Complex forms and validation, reusable UI components, build optimization, code reviews and joint codebase refactoring.

Stack: React, TypeScript, Redux Toolkit, PrimeReact, Webpack

block1.name: Bauman Moscow State Technical University
block1.desc: Computer science and applied mathematics: algorithms and data structures, OOP and SOLID, networking protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, WebSocket, REST), asynchronicity and the Event Loop, performance fundamentals.

block2.name: Gymnasium №7
block2.desc: Physics & mathematics track: programming fundamentals (C++, Python), participation in math and CS olympiads.

projects.text1:
The B2B admin panel of Yandex 360 for Business is a large team product (a frontend team of 15+ engineers) that has existed for years. I'm one of the developers; below is what I personally owned.

Main work (~85% of my time) — product projects that grow functionality and business money:
- Large cross-service features turnkey: new registration flows, add-on activation, referral and promo mechanics
- I own features end-to-end: frontend + server-side SSR/BFF logic (Node.js) + integrations with adjacent services
- Cut TTM for new locales by 5–7% and Dev Time by 20–40% via modular refactoring
- Contributions to the corporate design system (components from scratch via Figma) and a large-scale UI migration to it
projects.text2:
Engineering initiatives (beyond product tasks, on my own):

1. static_override — debug local frontend static directly on a real prestable pod: a built pod serves static from my dev machine, no rebuild or redeploy; cut the debug loop dramatically
2. Moved frequently-changing data out of the release cycle: updated without a release, reused across 4+ features
3. Sped up the team's PR flow: test-stand provisioning −19% (p50) / −30% (p90), ~2400 hrs/year saved
4. Performance: a heavy SSR endpoint 2.9s → 200ms (−90%), higher APDEX
5. Dashboards (ClickHouse + DataLens), a talk at an internal conference, ESLint rules from an analysis of 2000+ reviews

Stack: React, TypeScript, Node.js (SSR/BFF), Redux, design system, CI/CD, Playwright/Jest, ClickHouse/DataLens

projects.text1:
Multi-page Next.js app (50+ pages) for a private investment club: member areas, analytics, content.

- Auth and role-based access (RBAC, JWT)
- i18n multilingual support: Russian / English / Estonian
- Dashboards and data visualization (Chart.js), interactive tables with filtering
- Component architecture: custom components + Material UI
- Performance: SSR, code splitting, caching (SWR)
projects.text2:
Role and results:

- Built the app's frontend from scratch and brought core flows to a working state
- Set up CI/CD (GitHub Actions, Docker, Nginx)
- Covered key logic with unit tests (Jest)
- Lighthouse 90+ on core metrics

Commercial project, stopped on the client's side.

Stack: Next.js, TypeScript, Redux Toolkit, RTK Query, Material UI, Chart.js, i18next, Jest, Docker, Nginx

projects.text1:
Cross-platform React Native app (Expo) — the mobile version of the investment club mirroring the web functionality.

- Single codebase for iOS and Android
- Auth and role-based access (RBAC, JWT) adapted for mobile
- Shared i18n config with the web version
- Charts and visualization (Victory Native), gestures, offline cache
projects.text2:
Technical tasks:

- Replacing web-specific libraries with mobile equivalents (Chart.js → Victory Native, Material UI → RN Paper)
- Optimization for large datasets and low-end devices
- Navigation (React Navigation), push notifications (Expo), biometrics, offline mode

Commercial project, stopped on the client's side.

Stack: React Native, Expo, TypeScript, Victory Native, React Navigation, Redux Toolkit, i18next, Jest

projects.text1:
This very portfolio site — a SPA on my own stack, no UI libraries:

- Architecture: Vite + React, 100% custom components (zero-dependency UI)
- State: React Context + type-safe hooks (no Redux)
- Performance: code splitting, tree shaking, lazy-loaded heavy assets, Lighthouse 95+
- i18n: dynamic language loading (RU/EN), SEO meta tags per language
- Interactivity: custom SwiperJS carousel, animations (Animate.css), pure-CSS parallax
- Deployment: static on GitHub Pages with automated CI/CD
projects.text2:
Technical details:

1. Build & deployment:
   - Vite build in seconds
   - CI/CD pipeline: lint + build + auto-deploy to GitHub Pages via GitHub Actions
   - HashRouter for correct SPA routing on static hosting

2. Architecture:
   - Feature-based structure, alias imports (@/)
   - State via React Context, no external stores

3. UX:
   - Adaptive grid system (no libraries)
   - Micro-animations and smooth page transitions

Stack: Vite 5, React 18, TypeScript, i18next, Animate.css, SwiperJS 11, GitHub Actions, GitHub Pages

projects.text1:
Next.js rental platform with full SSR and:

- Architecture: 100% server-side data (except PATCH/POST)
- UI Kit: Deeply customized Mantine UI
- Image optimizations:
  - Custom SwiperJS (v11) carousel
  - Next.js Image + lazy loading
  - Content priority loading
- Security: Google Authenticator for admin (TOTP)
- Validation: Zod + React Hook Form schemas
- SEO: 95+ Lighthouse (SSR + dynamic meta tags)
projects.text2:
Admin system & tech details:

1. Secure admin panel:
   - TOTP auth (google-authenticator)
   - Short-TTL JWT tokens
   - Custom data validation (including files)

2. Optimizations:
   - Dynamic SwiperJS import
   - Critical route prefetching
   - API response caching (stale-while-revalidate)

3. Integrations:
   - Tabler Icons (custom bundle)
   - Server Actions for mutations
   - Streaming SSR for slow connections

4. Implementation:
   - Zero client-side state (server cache)
   - Progressive enhancement for old browsers
   - Full TypeScript strict mode

Stack: Next.js 14 (App Router), Mantine UI 7, SwiperJS 11, Zod, React Hook Form, Google Authenticator, Tabler Icons, Turbopack

projects.text1:
High-performance Next.js landing with full infra:

- Next.js 14 SSR for instant loading
- Auto-generated SEO meta tags
- Media optimizations:
  - Compressed WebP images
  - Below-the-fold lazy loading
- Full infrastructure:
  - Docker containerization
  - Nginx reverse proxy with HTTP/2
  - Let's Encrypt via Certbot
- CI/CD pipeline:
  - GitHub Actions auto-deploys
  - Pre-deploy build checks
  - Dependency caching
projects.text2:
Technical implementation:

1. Performance:
   - 100/100 Google PageSpeed
   - TTFB < 200ms via SSR
   - Optimized web fonts

2. Security:
   - Auto SSL renewal
   - CSP security headers
   - Nginx DDoS rate-limiting

3. DevOps:
   - Multi-stage Docker builds
   - Container health checks
   - Service auto-recovery

Stack: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Docker 24, Nginx 1.25, Certbot 2.8, CI/CD (GitHub Actions)

projects.text1:
A single-page CV app in React + Vite with in-browser PDF generation:

- Layout strictly fit to A4, CSS Modules, no heavy UI libraries
- Client-side PDF generation (pdf-lib) — print and download without a backend
- Internationalization: i18n (RU/EN)
- Performance: Lighthouse 100, minimal bundle
- Deployment: GitHub Pages
projects.text2:
Why: a quick pet project to assemble a clean PDF resume straight from code and to practice client-side document generation and print-ready layout.

Stack: React 18, Vite 5, TypeScript, pdf-lib, i18next

workExampleModal.ndaNotice: The product UI is under NDA, so screenshots aren't published here. The product is publicly available at admin.yandex.ru.
